summary: Gruber examines Pickle, a startup accepting $800 pre-orders for AR glasses, and argues convincingly that it exhibits hallmarks of a scam and a cult. He highlights how the company quietly changed its FAQ from describing the Pickle 1 as a 'standalone device' to a 'phone-tethered personal intelligence interface' after criticism, which undermines their original pitch. He points out a critical technical flaw: the claimed phone-tethering functionality is literally impossible on iPhone due to iOS background processing restrictions. The company's CEO's rebuttal relies on NDA handwaving and contradicts their own earlier marketing. Gruber is particularly alarmed by the revelation that the team lives together in a house and works seven days a week, combined with the product's pseudo-spiritual 'Soul Computer' branding.
key_points: Pickle quietly rewrote its FAQ to change the product description from 'standalone device' to 'phone-tethered personal intelligence interface' after public criticism
The claimed phone-tethering functionality is technically impossible on iPhone because iOS doesn't allow background tethering software
The CEO's rebuttal relies on NDA claims to explain why they can't demonstrate their own hardware
The company's living-and-working-together arrangement combined with spiritual product language ('Soul Computer', 'a new soul') resembles cult dynamics
The pattern of impossible claims, inability to provide evidence, and shifting explanations follows a classic scam playbook
money_quotes: Park's rebuttal, in my opinion, boils down to (a) a bunch of handwaving about Pickle conveniently not being able to explain their own hardware because of NDAs.
Which to me already nullifies the entire premise of the fantasy device they showed at launch.
That can't be true if the user carries an iPhone because there's no way Pickle can run tethering software in the background on iOS.
I do not believe it'll do even 1/100th of what they claim when paired to an Android phone, either. But it's literally impossible with an iPhone.
Pickle doesn't sound a little like a cult. It sounds exactly like a cult.
Impossible claims. A compound where 'employees' live and work seven days a week. Spiritual mumbo jumbo.
The only way Pickle could sound more like a cult would be if their 'employees' (are any of them actually getting paid?) all shaved their heads and wore Hare Krishna-style robes.
If you work there, be wary of the Kool-Aid.
key_insight: Pickle's pattern of impossible technical claims, quietly rewritten marketing copy, NDA-based deflection, and a live-in workforce pitching a 'Soul Computer' adds up to something between a scam and a cult.
